q: What is an example of a customer pain point in banking and how does a fintech address this?
lead: A common pain point is consumers not knowing their credit score; fintechs like Credit Karma and Borrowell address it by giving away free credit scores and reports.
points: Credit scores from Equifax and TransUnion measure default risk yet most consumers are unaware of theirs.
Credit Karma's free app attracted 4 million users in four years and became profitable.
In 2019 an estimated 7 million US households were unbanked, lacking checking or savings accounts.
Fintechs deliver an experience that is easier, cheaper, faster, and more convenient.

q: How did the 2008-2009 Global Financial Crisis lower the barriers to entry for fintechs into banking?
lead: The crisis lowered the two main hurdles of trust and regulation, destroying public confidence in banks and prompting policies that encouraged new entrants.
points: A 2012 Guardian survey found 71% thought banks had not learned their lesson.
Bailouts and recession spurred open banking legislation forcing banks to share customer data.
Politicians eased or ignored regulatory requirements for fintech start-ups to boost competition.
Disgruntled industry insiders left to launch fintech start-ups draining incumbent profit pools.

q: What is the value proposition of a fintech app developer and their strategy for building and scaling their business?
lead: Fintech app developers provide an innovative, inexpensive solution to a specific pain point, then unbundle a profitable bank product and later rebundle complementary services.
points: Success requires simple onboarding, intuitive UX, strong security, and a personalized experience.
Unbundling offers a better single product at lower cost, avoiding bank cross-subsidies and loss leaders.
Establish a foothold with early adopters like millennials, then rebundle to make customers sticky.
Fits Christensen's disruptive innovation: start cheaper and good enough, then move up-market.

q: Why do fintechs want to adopt a B2B model and white-label their mobile apps to incumbents?
lead: B2C customer acquisition is very costly ($500 to $1,000 per customer), so many fintechs pivot to B2B, licensing white-labeled apps to banks that have large customer bases.
points: Incumbents have deep pockets, regulatory expertise, and are perfect customers held back by legacy IT.
White-label means the bank's brand replaces the fintech's, as with Sensibill and Moven.
SaaS licensing brings stable recurring revenues attractive to shareholders and a path to exit.
Banks gain innovative solutions without developing them in-house.

q: What is a challenger bank and how is it different from a traditional bricks-and-mortar bank?
lead: A challenger bank (also neobank or digital bank) offers the same banking products as bricks-and-mortar banks but delivers an entirely electronic, end-to-end digital experience.
points: Account opening takes under 10 minutes on mobile with no branch visit, though KYC ID upload is required.
The bank is open 24/7, 365 days, with intuitive dashboards, gamification, and chatbots.
Value proposition is lower cost, transparent fees, fast service, and better customer experience.
Initially appealed to millennials and Gen Z who valued convenience and speed.

q: How are challenger banks doing around the world and who are some leading players?
lead: Challenger banks are thriving, with over 300 globally and fastest growth in Brazil, India, and the US; leaders include Revolut, N26, Nubank, and Chime.
points: Four leading banks together served 111 million customers as of year-end 2022.
COVID-19 accelerated adoption; app downloads worldwide exceeded 264 million, led by Latin America.
Nubank had 75 million customers by 2022; Revolut was valued at US$33 billion.
Growth was strongest in markets with many underbanked and unbanked consumers.

q: What has been the go-to-market strategy of challenger banks and are they regulated?
lead: Challenger banks build a foothold with one product in their home market then rebundle and expand abroad; regulation splits between those holding a banking license and those without.
points: N26 started with a free deposit account and debit card; Revolut began as a licensed MSB targeting travelers.
A banking license is needed for insured deposits and central bank support, but loans and credit cards are unregulated.
Unlicensed banks like Chime must partner with licensed banks (Bancorp Bank, Stride Bank).
Many expand into neighboring countries and partner with fintechs via APIs to add products.

q: What is the business model and monetization strategy of a financial marketplace?
lead: Financial marketplaces are multi-sided platforms that connect customers with third-party products via APIs and open banking, monetizing through fees, commissions, and advertising.
points: They offer full-service-bank variety without the capital and regulation, building network effects.
Fees or commissions are charged to the money-side third parties selling products.
Credit Karma gave away free credit scores then sold products, acquired by Intuit for $7 billion.
Borrowell and Fidor Market pivoted to bundle third-party financial products for their users.

q: What are some of the strategies adopted by incumbents in response to fintech new entrants?
lead: Incumbents choose from a menu of options depending on capabilities and scale: build in-house, R&D labs, partner, license, invest, acquire, or target fintechs as customers.
points: Bigger players build apps in-house (Goldman's Marcus) or set up innovation labs, risking innovation theater.
Partnerships include Canada's Interac e-Transfers and the US banks' Zelle joint venture against Venmo.
Banks license fintech software (JPMorgan with Plaid) or buy equity via corporate VC arms like RBC Ventures.
Acquisitions like JPMorgan-WePay can succeed while BPCE-Fidor failed from culture clash.

q: What is banking-as-a-service (BaaS) and what are the advantages for both the fintech and the bank partner?
lead: BaaS lets a licensed bank provide the regulated back office so fintechs offer white-labeled banking products, letting each side focus on its strengths.
points: The bank holds deposits, underwrites loans, and satisfies capital, KYC, and AML requirements.
The fintech looks and acts like a bank while focusing on the customer technology experience.
Smaller regional banks like WebBank, Bancorp, and Cross River seized this opportunity over giants.
Customers gain FDIC-insured deposits and global payments; WebBank served Klarna and LendingClub.
